content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Henry L. Smith, Jr. 
The El Paso Company 
P.O. Box 2185 
Houston, Texas 
77001 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
March 23, 1981 
I 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
This in in response to your letter of March 4, 1981. 
Your 
specific questions are: 
(1) 
must a political action committee report the receipt 
of a late contribution, and 
(2) 
what kind of an election is defined as a special 
election for the purpose of determining whether or 
not a special election campaign statement is required. 
In regards to your first question, a late contribution is defined 
by Section 82036 of the Political Act as " .•. any contribution 
including a loan which totals in the aggregate one thousand dollars 
($1000) or more that is made to or received by a candidate, a 
controlled committee, or a committee formed or existing primarily 
to support or oppose a measure before the date of the election at 
which the candidate or measure is being voted on but after the 
closing date of the last campaign statement required to be filed 
before the election". 
Therefore, a political action committee 
(i.e., recipient committee) is not required to file a late contri-
bution report detailing late contributions received by the politi-
cal action committee unless it is controlled by or primarily formed 
to support or oppose a candidate or measure. 
Your second question is in regards to the filing of special election 
campaign statements. 
A special election as defined in the 
California Elections Code Section 27 is " ... an election, the 
specific time for the holding of which is not prescribed by law". 
Therefore, city or county elections normally scheduled would not 
be defined as special elections and a special election campaign 
statement would never be required by a political action committee 


-2-
contributing to candidates or measures in a normally scheduled 
local election. 
If you have any further questions, please feel free to contact 
me. 
Yours very truly, 
-~~ 
Lynn Montgomery 
Political Reform Consultant 


THE 
1:1 PaSD COMPANY 
Ms. Lynn Montgomery 
California Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear Ms. Montgomery; 
2727 ALLEN PARKWAY 
P O. BOX 2185 
HOUSTON. TEXAS 77001 
PHONE 713-525-9400 
March 4, 1981 
As we discussed at the seminar in Los Angeles, I would appreciate 
answers to two questions concerning the recent changes in the Political 
Reform Act. 
First, must political action committees (PACs) report late contri-
butions coming into them? 
I understand that a PAC would have to report 
a late contribution made by it. 
Looking at the new § 82036 and reading 
it in two parts separatedby the word "or", one reads " .•. ($1,000) or 
more that is made to ... a candidate, a controlled committee, or a com-
mittee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose a candidate or 
measure ... " 
This part would not include a late contribution coming into 
a PAC. 
Reading the material on the other side of the word "or", one--
reads " ... ($1,000) or more that is ... received by a candidate, a 
controlled committee or a committee formed or existing primarily to 
support or oppose ••. " 
This would not include money coming into a PAC 
either. 
The extensive language changes in this section suggest that the 
intention of the change was to make clear that PACs need not report late 
contributions coming into them. 
Indeed, federal law was recently changed 
to remove the requirement that the PAC report late contributions coming 
into it. 
Secondly, what kind of election triggers the special election 
campaign statements for PACs? 
The new 1981 Information Manual on page 
13, Section G says that a special election means "an election which is 
not held on a normally scheduled date" and you stated at the seminar 
that this was interpreted to mean a specially called (i.e. ad hoc) 
election, instead of, for example, city elections normally scheduled in 


Ms. Lynn Montgomery 
March 4, 1981 
Page 2 
the spring. 
However, the new statute at § 842l0(a) could be read to 
mean simply that the election triggering such reports is an election 
held on a date other than the first Tuesday after the first Monday in 
June or November. 
In other words, in the statute the phrase "held on a 
date other than the first Tuesday •.• " could be read to define the 
preceding term "a special election". 
Did the Legislature intend to 
exclude from the § 84210 election reporting requirements all elections 
which were regularly scheduled but scheduled on dates other than the 
first Tuesday after the first Monday in June or November? 
Note also 
that the statute does not mention even or odd numbered years in con-
nection with the above June and November dates. 
What about an election 
that might be held on June 2, 1981 or November 3, 1981? 
Hould such an 
election trigger a report under § 842l0? 
Compare § 842l0(a) with § 
84200(c) to which it is linked. 
Section § 84200(c) also does not 
mention odd or even numbered years, and if it is read together with § 
842l0(a), the conclusion is strengthened that a report is triggered 
under § 842l0(a) if the election is merely held on a day other than the 
first Tuesday etc., even though the election is a regularly scheduled 
election. 
I would appreciate answers to these two questions, as soon as 
possible. 
Thank you for your help. 
mma 
Sincerely, 
Henry 1. 'Smith, Jr. 
Attorney

sections.question: (1) must a political action committee report the receipt of a late contribution, and (2) what kind of an election is defined as a special election for the purpose of determining whether or not a special election campaign statement is required.
sections.conclusion: A political action committee (i.e., recipient committee) is not required to file a late contribution report detailing late contributions received by the political action committee unless it is controlled by or primarily formed to support or oppose a candidate or measure. A special election as defined in the California Elections Code Section 27 is an election the specific time for the holding of which is not prescribed by law. Therefore, city or county elections normally scheduled would not be defined as special elections and a special election campaign statement would never be required by a political action committee contributing to candidates or measures in a normally scheduled local election.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquirer asks whether PACs must report late contributions received by them, and what constitutes a 'special election' under California law for purposes of triggering special election campaign statement filing requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: PACs need not report late contributions received unless the PAC is controlled by or primarily formed to support/oppose a candidate or measure. A special election is one held at a time not prescribed by law, so normally scheduled local elections do not qualify as special elections requiring special campaign statements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (March 4, 1981 letter from Smith) and FPPC response (March 23, 1981 letter from Montgomery). Extracted information from the FPPC response letter. Both questions and conclusions are clearly delineated in the response.

QUESTION: (1) must a political action committee report the receipt of a late contribution, and (2) what kind of an election is defined as a special election for the purpose of determining whether or not a special election campaign statement is required.

CONCLUSION: A political action committee (i.e., recipient committee) is not required to file a late contribution report detailing late contributions received by the political action committee unless it is controlled by or primarily formed to support or oppose a candidate or measure. A special election as defined in the California Elections Code Section 27 is an election the specific time for the holding of which is not prescribed by law. Therefore, city or county elections normally scheduled would not be defined as special elections and a special election campaign statement would never be required by a political action committee contributing to candidates or measures in a normally scheduled local election.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to inquiries from El Paso Company regarding late contribution reporting requirements for PACs and the definition of special elections under California's Political Reform Act. The Commission clarifies that PACs are not required to report late contributions received and that special elections are only those held at non-prescribed times, excluding regularly scheduled local elections.
